name_local: Palais de justice du comté de Fayette

phrases.intent_en: Ask where a specific office is
phrases.text_local: Excusez-moi, où se trouve le bureau des permis ?
phrases.pronunciation: ex-kyoo-ZAY-mwah, oo suh TROOV luh boo-ROH day pair-MEE
phrases.cultural_note: French civic buildings ('bâtiments administratifs') are organized similarly to American ones. Using 'bureau' (office) is correct and natural — French bureaucracy is famously detailed, so clear questions are valued.

responses.french: C'est au deuxième étage, couloir B.
responses.english: It's on the second floor, corridor B.
responses.phonetic: say oh duh-ZYEM ay-TAHJ, koo-LWAR BAY

responses.french: Prenez l'ascenseur et tournez à droite.
responses.english: Take the elevator and turn right.
responses.phonetic: pruh-NAY lah-son-SEUR ay toor-NAY ah DRWAT

phrases.text_local: Bonjour, j'ai rendez-vous à dix heures.
phrases.cultural_note: 'Rendez-vous' — a French word adopted wholesale into English — means both a meeting and an appointment. Using it in its original French context feels perfectly natural and slightly elegant.

responses.french: Très bien. Puis-je voir votre pièce d'identité ?
responses.english: Very good. May I see your ID?
responses.phonetic: tray BYAN. pwee-juh VWAR vo-truh PYAS dee-don-tee-TAY

responses.french: Veuillez vous asseoir, on va vous appeler.
responses.english: Please have a seat, someone will call you.
responses.phonetic: vuh-YAY voo zah-SWAR, on vah voo zah-puh-LAY

phrases.text_local: Y a-t-il un parking à proximité ?
phrases.pronunciation: ee ah-TEEL un PAR-keeng ah prox-ee-mee-TAY
phrases.cultural_note: The French word 'parking' (borrowed from English) is universal and understood everywhere. Asking about parking near government buildings is a very practical, common exchange in any French city.

responses.french: Oui, il y a un parking public à deux pâtés de maisons.
responses.english: Yes, there's a public parking lot two blocks away.
responses.phonetic: wee, eel ee ah un PAR-keeng poob-LEEK ah duh pah-TAY duh may-ZON

phrases.intent_en: Comment that the building is historic
phrases.text_local: Ce bâtiment est vraiment impressionnant. Il doit dater du XIXe siècle.
phrases.pronunciation: suh bah-tee-MON ay vray-MON an-press-yo-NON. eel dwah dah-TAY doo DEZ-nuh-VEM SYEK-luh
phrases.cultural_note: The French have a strong culture of architectural heritage ('patrimoine architectural'). Remarking on a building's age and grandeur is a natural and appreciated observation — it shows you're paying attention.

phrases.intent_en: Ask for directions to the clerk's office
phrases.text_local: Pourriez-vous m'indiquer le bureau de l'officier d'état civil, s'il vous plaît ?
phrases.pronunciation: poo-RYAY-voo man-dee-KAY luh boo-ROH duh lo-fees-YAY day-TAH see-VEEL, seel voo PLAY
phrases.cultural_note: 'L'état civil' in France handles birth, marriage, and death records — the equivalent of a county clerk's office. The phrasing is formal because civic offices in France respond well to formal register.

responses.french: Le bureau de l'état civil est au rez-de-chaussée, porte numéro trois.
responses.english: The civil registry office is on the ground floor, door number three.
responses.phonetic: luh boo-ROH duh lay-TAH see-VEEL ay oh ray-duh-SHOH-say, PORT noo-may-ROH TRWAH
